excerpt: Stratum V2 introduces a job-negotiation subprotocol that allows individual miners or mining devices to construct their own block templates while continuing to receive payouts from a pool. It also adds end-to-end encryption to the miner-pool channel, eliminating the man-in-the-middle hijacks that have repeatedly extracted hashrate from V1 farms.
body:
Source excerpt:
Stratum V2 introduces a job-negotiation subprotocol that allows individual miners or mining devices to construct their own block templates while continuing to receive payouts from a pool. It also adds end-to-end encryption to the miner-pool channel, eliminating the man-in-the-middle hijacks that have repeatedly extracted hashrate from V1 farms.

---

Cited by leaf "Pool dynamics and Stratum V2":
A miner running a single ASIC has to wait years between blocks on average even at modern difficulty, so almost all hashrate joins a pool: many miners contribute hashrate, the pool finds blocks roughly proportionally to its total share, and rewards get split out per contributed work [1]. The economics of variance reduction are real and not going away — the only question is what the pool operator decides on behalf of its miners, and that question has been the active reform area for the last few years.

Under Stratum V1 — the protocol most pools still use — the pool operator builds the block template: which transactions go in, in what order, and which addresses receive the coinbase and miner-fee outputs. Individual miners just hash whatever the pool sends them. This concentrates a real censorship and policy lever in the hands of a few dozen pool operators worldwide [2]. The well-publicized incidents of pools temporarily filtering specific transactions — for sanctions compliance, for spam policy, or under regulatory pressure — all played out at this layer.

Stratum V2 (BIP started 2019, now in production rollout) splits the protocol so individual miners can construct their own block templates and submit work against them, while the pool still handles payment accounting [3]. The pool is reduced to a payment processor; the policy choices about which transactions get included move back to whoever owns the hashrate. Adoption is partial and gradual — Braiins, Demand, and a handful of pools have shipped V2 endpoints; most hashrate still mines under V1 — but the architectural fix to the most-cited centralization concern is built and deployed, not theoretical.
